content.text: Large language models are trained on a fixed snapshot of text and their knowledge is baked into billions of floating-point weights at training time. Once training ends, the model knows nothing about events, documents, or facts that appeared after its

. It also knows nothing about private data — your company's internal wiki, your database of customer support tickets, or a PDF that arrived in your inbox this morning. Ask it about any of these and it will either say

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) solves this by adding a retrieval step before generation. Instead of relying on the model's parametric memory, the system fetches relevant documents from an external knowledge store at query time and injects them into the prompt. The LLM then answers by reading the retrieved text — grounded in real, up-to-date, verifiable documents.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison: Without RAG, the LLM has a frozen knowledge cutoff and produces hallucinated answers with no citation. With RAG, a retriever fetches real documents from a knowledge base and injects them into the prompt, so the LLM produces a grounded answer with a source citation.

content.text: The Core Problem: What LLMs Cannot Do Alone

Every LLM has a training data cutoff — a date after which it has seen nothing. GPT-4 Turbo, for example, has a knowledge cutoff of April 2024. Asking it about a product launched in October 2024 will produce either a refusal or a plausible-sounding fabrication. RAG removes this constraint entirely: the knowledge base can be updated continuously, and every retrieval fetch reflects the current state of the indexed documents.
When an LLM is asked about something it does not know, it does not reliably say
. Instead it generates a plausible continuation — the

problem. Hallucinations occur because the model's objective during training was to predict the next token, not to be accurate. A confident, grammatically correct wrong answer and a correct answer look identical to the loss function if both are well-formed text.

The hallucination rate varies dramatically by task. For factual questions about obscure entities, hallucination rates of 20–40% are common in baseline LLMs. RAG reduces this by grounding the answer in retrieved source text — the model is prompted to read and summarise a real document rather than recall from memory.
when the answer is in the prompt context, the model attends to that context far more strongly than to its parametric weights. The attention mechanism's
effect means that a well-retrieved passage almost always dominates the response over the model's prior beliefs, especially in instruction-tuned models.

Public LLMs are trained on public data. An enterprise chatbot that needs to answer questions about internal HR policies, proprietary product specifications, or confidential legal documents cannot rely on parametric knowledge. Fine-tuning is one option but requires expensive re-training every time documents change. RAG provides a cheaper, faster alternative: index the private documents once, keep the index current, and the model automatically retrieves from them at query time without any weight updates.

Parametric knowledge answers cannot cite sources — the model absorbed the information during training and can no longer tell you which document it came from. RAG returns the actual source documents alongside the answer, enabling the application to show the user where the answer came from and allow them to verify it independently.
At its core, RAG adds one step before generation:
chunk documents, embed them into vectors, store in a vector database.
at query time, embed the query and find the top-K most relevant chunks.
concatenate the retrieved chunks with the user query to form a
send the augmented prompt to the LLM; the model reads the context and generates a grounded answer.

# Minimal RAG pipeline in Python
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA

# 1. Index: embed documents and store
documents = [
    Document(page_content="Acme Corp Q3 2025 revenue was $2.1B, up 8% YoY.",
             metadata={"source": "acme-10q-q3-2025.pdf", "page": 4}),
    Document(page_content="Acme Corp announced Project Titan on Oct 2025.",
             metadata={"source": "acme-press-release-oct25.pdf", "page": 1}),
]
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
store = Chroma.from_documents(documents, embeddings)

# 2+3+4. Retrieve → Augment → Generate (wrapped by RetrievalQA)
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", temperature=0)
qa = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(
    llm=llm,
    retriever=store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 3}),
    return_source_documents=True,
)

response = qa.invoke({"query": "What was Acme Corp Q3 2025 revenue?"})
print(response["result"])
# "Acme Corp reported Q3 2025 revenue of $2.1B, up 8% YoY."

for doc in response["source_documents"]:
    print("Source:", doc.metadata["source"], "p.", doc.metadata["page"])
# Source: acme-10q-q3-2025.pdf p. 4
The model never needed to know this fact parametrically — it read it from the retrieved document and cited the source.
Why RAG Works: The Grounding Argument
The effectiveness of RAG rests on a well-understood property of the transformer attention mechanism:
when the two are in conflict.
Formally, a language model computes a conditional probability over the next token given context

The version conditioned on retrieved context is almost always more accurate for factual questions than the version relying on parametric knowledge alone, provided the retrieval step surfaces genuinely relevant documents. This is why even retrieval of moderate quality produces significant hallucination reduction.

A key insight from the original RAG paper (Lewis et al., 2020) is that the retriever and the generator can be trained jointly or independently — even a simple BM25 retriever paired with a large LLM outperforms the LLM alone on knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.
When RAG Is the Right Tool
RAG is particularly effective when the task is
— when the quality of the answer depends on having access to specific facts rather than on reasoning or creativity. Canonical use cases:
answering employee questions about HR policies, IT documentation, or project history from internal wikis and PDFs.
resolving support tickets by retrieving from product manuals, known-issue databases, and past ticket resolutions.
answering questions about regulatory requirements by retrieving from statute text, case law, and internal compliance documents.
summarising a paper or comparing multiple papers by retrieving from a corpus of academic publications.
answering questions about a 200-page contract or technical specification that exceeds the model's context window.
When RAG Is Not the Answer
RAG is not a universal solution. There are task types where it adds complexity without improving quality:
the model's ability to solve equations or prove theorems comes from parametric learning, not document retrieval. RAG cannot retrieve a
generating a poem or a story does not require facts from a knowledge base. RAG adds overhead without benefit.
if the user provides the full spec in the prompt, there is nothing to retrieve. Context is already in the prompt.
Fast-moving knowledge with no indexing lag tolerance:
if documents change every second (live stock prices, sensor readings), you need a different architecture — tool use with a live API call, not vector retrieval.
Questions answerable by the model's parametric knowledge alone:
asking a 70B model to explain bubble sort does not benefit from RAG; the knowledge is already in the weights. Adding retrieval only increases latency.

use RAG when the answer requires a specific fact, document, or piece of data that the model cannot reliably recall from training. Skip RAG when the task is a reasoning, creative, or well-known general knowledge problem the model handles confidently.
RAG vs Fine-Tuning: A Brief Comparison
A common question is whether RAG or fine-tuning is the better way to inject domain knowledge into an LLM. They address different failure modes:
Yes — for facts in the index

In practice, RAG and fine-tuning are complementary. A fine-tuned model that has learned the right output format and tone, combined with RAG that provides up-to-date facts, outperforms either approach alone.
RAG was introduced by Lewis et al. in the 2020 paper
Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

(NeurIPS 2020). The original formulation jointly fine-tuned a BART generator and a DPR (Dense Passage Retriever). The retriever index was frozen during training; retrieval and generation were trained end-to-end with marginalisation over retrieved documents:

attrs.latex: p(y \mid x) = \sum_{z \in \text{top-K}(p_\eta(z|x))} p_\eta(z \mid x) \cdot p_\theta(y \mid x, z)

content.text: is the generator. Modern RAG systems simplify this by treating retrieval and generation as independent stages (RAG-Sequence or RAG-Token), making them easier to build with off-the-shelf retrievers and LLMs.
Minimal Working Example: The RAG Loop
Understanding RAG requires understanding what happens at each step when a query arrives:

import numpy as np

# Simulated corpus (in production: vector database with millions of chunks)
corpus = [
    {"id": "doc-1", "text": "The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889.", "embedding": None},
    {"id": "doc-2", "text": "Paris is the capital of France.",          "embedding": None},
    {"id": "doc-3", "text": "France has a population of ~68 million.",  "embedding": None},
]

def embed(text: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """Stub — in production, call an embedding model API."""
    # Real: return openai_client.embeddings.create(input=text).data[0].embedding
    return np.random.rand(1536)    # placeholder

def cosine_sim(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-9))

# ── Step 1: Index ──
for doc in corpus:
    doc["embedding"] = embed(doc["text"])

# ── Step 2: Retrieve ──
def retrieve(query: str, k: int = 2) -> list[dict]:
    q_emb = embed(query)
    scored = [(cosine_sim(q_emb, doc["embedding"]), doc) for doc in corpus]
    return [doc for _, doc in sorted(scored, reverse=True)[:k]]

# ── Step 3: Augment ──
def build_prompt(query: str, retrieved_docs: list[dict]) -> str:
    context = "\n\n".join(f"[{doc['id']}] {doc['text']}" for doc in retrieved_docs)
    return (
        f"Answer the question using only the context below.\n\n"
        f"Context:\n{context}\n\n"
        f"Question: {query}\n"
        f"Answer:"
    )

# ── Step 4: Generate ──
query = "When was the Eiffel Tower built?"
docs = retrieve(query, k=2)
prompt = build_prompt(query, docs)
print(prompt)
# Context:
# [doc-1] The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889.
# [doc-2] Paris is the capital of France.
#
# Question: When was the Eiffel Tower built?
# Answer:
#
# → LLM reads this and responds: "The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889. [doc-1]"

Before deploying RAG, measure whether it actually helps. The RAGAS framework (RAG Assessment) provides three orthogonal metrics that together characterise RAG quality without requiring ground-truth answers for every question:

does the generated answer contain only claims that are supported by the retrieved context? A faithful answer makes no claim beyond what the retrieved passages state. Measured by checking each claim in the answer against the context.
does the generated answer actually address the user's question? An answer can be faithful (fully grounded) yet irrelevant (answering a different question than was asked).
did the retriever surface all the passages needed to answer the question? Low context recall means the retriever is the bottleneck — the generator cannot fix missing context.

# pip install ragas langchain-openai
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall
from datasets import Dataset

# Prepare evaluation set (question, answer, contexts, ground_truth)
eval_data = {
    "question": [
        "What was Acme Corp Q3 2025 revenue?",
        "When did Acme Corp announce Project Titan?",
    ],
    "answer": [
        "Acme Corp reported Q3 2025 revenue of $2.1B, up 8% YoY.",
        "Acme Corp announced Project Titan in October 2025.",
    ],
    "contexts": [
        ["Acme Corp Q3 2025 revenue was $2.1B, up 8% YoY."],
        ["Acme Corp announced Project Titan on Oct 2025."],
    ],
    "ground_truth": [
        "Q3 2025 revenue was $2.1 billion.",
        "Project Titan was announced October 2025.",
    ],
}
dataset = Dataset.from_dict(eval_data)

result = evaluate(
    dataset=dataset,
    metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall],
)
print(result)
# {'faithfulness': 0.97, 'answer_relevancy': 0.93, 'context_recall': 0.95}

A faithfulness score below 0.8 indicates the model is adding information not grounded in the retrieved text — a hallucination signal. A context recall below 0.7 means the retriever is missing relevant passages and should be improved before tuning the generator.
RAG adds latency relative to a direct LLM call. Understanding the latency breakdown helps you set a realistic SLA and identify where to optimise:
Smaller embedding model; batch if possible
Streaming; smaller model for simple queries
Retrieval adds ~15–70 ms to LLM time

For a sub-second user-facing latency target, the LLM generation step dominates. Retrieval is fast; the typical RAG overhead over a bare LLM call is only 15–70 ms — well below the threshold users notice. Use streaming responses (
) to show the first token as soon as the LLM starts generating, hiding most of the generation latency.
Skipping RAG for a task that needs it.

The most common mistake is leaving the LLM to answer from parametric memory for questions about internal documents or recent events. The result is confident hallucinations that are hard to detect without domain expertise.
Adding RAG to tasks that don't need it.
RAG adds latency (embedding, retrieval, context injection). For tasks well within the model's parametric knowledge, this is pure overhead with no quality gain.
Expecting RAG to fix all hallucinations.

RAG only helps if the relevant information is in the index and the retriever surfaces it. If a query asks about something not indexed, or the retriever returns irrelevant chunks, the model will still hallucinate. RAG reduces, not eliminates, hallucination.
The generator is only as good as the retrieved context. A poor retriever — wrong chunks, low recall — can make RAG worse than the vanilla LLM by injecting irrelevant text that distracts the model.

Fine-tuning teaches the model a new skill or output style; RAG provides it with facts. Using RAG when you actually need the model to behave differently (different tone, format, reasoning style) will not help.
Start with a clear answer to: what does my LLM not know?

If the answer is private documents or recent data, RAG is the right tool. If the answer is a specific reasoning capability, RAG will not help — look at fine-tuning or chain-of-thought prompting instead.

The quality of the RAG system is gated by the quality of the retriever. Better chunking strategy, better embedding model, and hybrid BM25+dense retrieval will improve final answers more than switching LLMs.
Always return source documents to the user.
One of RAG's main advantages is attribution. Display the sources alongside the answer so users can verify the claim and build trust in the system.
Set up an evaluation loop early.

Measure hallucination rate and answer accuracy on a labelled test set before shipping. Common metrics: faithfulness (does the answer match the retrieved context?), answer relevance (does the answer address the question?), context recall (did the retriever surface the relevant passage?).
Use a zero-temperature model for factual RAG.
makes the model's output more deterministic and faithful to the retrieved context. Creative temperature settings cause the model to drift from the source text.
RAG solves the knowledge cutoff, hallucination, and private-data problems
by fetching relevant documents at query time and injecting them into the LLM prompt.
The core loop is: Index → Retrieve → Augment → Generate.
Each step is independently improvable — better chunking, better retriever, better prompt, better LLM.
Grounding works because in-context information dominates parametric memory:
when the answer is in the prompt, the model attends to it over its weights.
— factual QA, document search, enterprise knowledge bases. It is not the right tool for reasoning, creative, or fast-changing live-data tasks.
: use RAG for facts and recency, fine-tuning for style, format, and task adaptation.
Retrieval quality is the primary lever
— invest in your chunking strategy, embedding model, and retrieval algorithm before optimising the generator.
